entries.openingLines:
Once upon a midnight dreary, while I pondered, weak and weary,
Over many a quaint and curious volume of forgotten lore—
While I nodded, nearly napping, suddenly there came a tapping,
As of some one gently rapping, rapping at my chamber door.
entries.title: The Raven
entries.author: Edgar Allan Poe

entries.openingLines:
I saw the best minds of my generation destroyed by madness, starving hysterical naked,
dragging themselves through the negro streets at dawn looking for an angry fix,
angelheaded hipsters burning for the ancient heavenly connection to the starry dynamo in the machinery of night,
entries.title: Howl
entries.author: Allen Ginsberg

entries.title: The Love Song of J. Alfred Prufrock
entries.author: T.S. Eliot

entries.openingLines:
Whose woods these are I think I know.
His house is in the village though;
He will not see me stopping here
To watch his woods fill up with snow.
entries.title: Stopping by Woods on a Snowy Evening
entries.author: Robert Frost

entries.openingLines:
Two roads diverged in a yellow wood,
And sorry I could not travel both
And be one traveler, long I stood
And looked down one as far as I could
To where it bent in the undergrowth;
entries.title: The Road Not Taken
entries.author: Robert Frost

entries.title: Do Not Go Gentle into That Good Night
entries.author: Dylan Thomas

entries.openingLines:
I met a traveller from an antique land,
Who said—"Two vast and trunkless legs of stone
Stand in the desert. . . .
entries.title: Ozymandias
entries.author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

entries.openingLines:
Shall I compare thee to a summer's day?
Thou art more lovely and more temperate:
entries.title: Shall I Compare Thee to a Summer's Day
entries.author: William Shakespeare

entries.openingLines:
"Hope" is the thing with feathers -
That perches in the soul -
And sings the tune without the words -
And never stops - at all -
entries.title: Hope is the Thing with Feathers
entries.author: Emily Dickinson

entries.openingLines:
Because I could not stop for Death –
He kindly stopped for me –
The Carriage held but just Ourselves –
And Immortality.
entries.title: Because I could not stop for Death
entries.author: Emily Dickinson

entries.openingLines:
Take this kiss upon the brow!
And, in parting from you now,
Thus much let me avow —
entries.title: A Dream Within a Dream
entries.author: Edgar Allan Poe

entries.openingLines:
It was many and many a year ago,
In a kingdom by the sea,
That a maiden there lived whom you may know
By the name of Annabel Lee;
entries.title: Annabel Lee
entries.author: Edgar Allan Poe

entries.openingLines:
If you can keep your head when all about you
    Are losing theirs and blaming it on you,
entries.title: If—
entries.author: Rudyard Kipling

entries.openingLines:
Turning and turning in the widening gyre
The falcon cannot hear the falconer;
entries.title: The Second Coming
entries.author: William Butler Yeats

entries.openingLines:
Something there is that doesn't love a wall,
That sends the frozen-ground-swell under it,
And spills the upper boulders in the sun;
And makes gaps even two can pass abreast.
entries.title: Mending Wall
entries.author: Robert Frost

entries.openingLines:
Tyger Tyger, burning bright,
In the forests of the night;
What immortal hand or eye,
Could frame thy fearful symmetry?
entries.title: The Tyger
entries.author: William Blake

entries.openingLines: What happens to a dream deferred?
entries.title: Harlem
entries.author: Langston Hughes

entries.openingLines:
April is the cruellest month, breeding
Lilacs out of the dead land, mixing
Memory and desire, stirring
Dull roots with spring rain.
entries.title: The Waste Land
entries.author: T.S. Eliot

entries.openingLines:
When you are old and grey and full of sleep,
And nodding by the fire, take down this book,
And slowly read, and dream of the soft look
Your eyes had once, and of their shadows deep;
entries.title: When You Are Old
entries.author: William Butler Yeats
